speaker: interviewer
text: --were just telling me a little bit about your life after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you came to Canada in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: '49. Can you tell me just a little bit about your life here in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we first came-- so we were very unhappy to start with, because it was strange. And-- and we were unhappy-- both of us. And mostly, we were unhappy because Ruda, who left literally all his-- we left on the 1st of November, 1948. We went to Switzerland. And we wait there. We waited for the visa, for the Canadian visa. And his class graduated in May or June of that '49. So he-- he was practically a doctor, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do the last four months in medical school? And there was no way he could get anywhere. Really, that was-- that was a heartbreak for him, and for both of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and my father decided that he would start a silverware factory like he started in Prague, in 1921. And he thought he would start where he finished off, you know-- basically. And he miscalculated. He shouldn't have done it. But that's neither here nor there. And Ruda, whatever they-- he took a partner-- and whatever they manufactured or did. So Ruda first was working for them as a silversmith, because he-- he did that in Prague a little bit. And then, he was working for them as a salesman, and all the time trying to get into medical school. And then I got-- I worked as a secretary. And my father was yelling at me, which was very unusual, because he was nervous. And then, I got pregnant. And-- and Dan was born on the 15th of April, 1950, which was wonderful, because it was the date of the liberation. And-- and when Dan was a year old-- a year and a 1/2 old, you know, Ruda was constantly sending applications and writing applications. And by that time, he knew there was a numerous clauses here. And he-- he didn't speak the language properly. And-- and they-- they just didn't want him. And they had no-- no way how to, they said, how to evaluate the University of Prague against the university here and the university that there. There was one disadvantage. The university, the Charles University of Prague, even though it was founded in 1346-- so it-- you can't really compare it with anything here. But it was closed from the 17th of December till '49, all through the war years. The Germans closed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they really stopped in 1939. But they-- here they stuck to, there was a war on. So anyway-- then finally, through a lot of coincidence, and maybe he told you himself how he got into Queens. And it was just wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Kingston with-- with a little boy who was 18 months old. And he was very precocious. And he was a joy. And-- and we made that and didn't think much of it. But then-- then it happened then the last year that I became pregnant with Peter, and-- but he managed to finish the studies. We had an apartment that was-- that would fit into this living room, I think. And-- but the last year, Danny went into a nursery. It was attached to Queens, and-- and the children were a great joy, really a great joy, and they-- and then, we moved to Montreal with a five-year-old and a four- month-old baby, who was gorgeous. He was blond with big blue eyes. His-- his hair, it turned dark only three, four years ago. But he still has big blue eyes. And they are nice boys. And we were in Montreal. And first, Ruda was in Ottawa. I was with them alone the last year. And then we-- he was in Montreal. And then he opened his practice. And it was a big success from the beginning. And then we bought a house and sold a house. And then, we bought a house. And then, we decided the boys are growing up too fast and we needed somebody else at home. So we had a little girl. It was fortunate that she was-- we wanted a little girl, but we would have accepted anybody. And so-- and life went on. The boy--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your little girl's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michelle. We call her Mischa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are the children today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Dan is in Nashville with his family. And Peter and Mischa are in Boston. And they are doing fine. And they have children. Dan has three-- two boys and a girl. The oldest boy is 19. And his name is Mark. And he's his second year at Carnegie Mellon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the second boy is Paul. And he is-- and his last-- first grade of high school. And Claire is twelve. And she is whatever grade-- nine? What-- what are you in grade twelve? Grade eight? Grade nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are-- the three of them are giants-- and dwarf me. Even the Claire is taller than I am. And-- but that's because her other grandparents are so tall. And Maisie's a wonderful daughter-in-law. Rosemary-- Maisie, who is finishing her-- on the 13th of December, she's graduating. And she's finishing her MBA. And everybody's very proud of her. And then, there is Peter who worked for MIT for 10 years. And now, he decided to open his own company, which is called Supply Works. And I still don't understand what they are doing. But they are-- it's something with computers and servicing. And-- and he is enjoying it tremendously. And he has a little girl called Daniella, who is five. And then, there is Mischa, who has-- her husband is Scott. And Scott, according to the-- to the whole family, to our whole family, is a prince of a person, because he can take Mischa under all weather, whether she is she it. And he loves her very much. He's a really-- he's a wonderful guy. And they have two children, Noah and Sasha. And Sasha is-- it's Sasha's birthday today. She's two years old today. And Noah is four. He'll be five in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does Mischa do? Other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Mischa is a-- has an MA from Harvard in counseling. And-- and now, she's a mommy. And Scott is-- he has an MBA. And he's working for a company that's called-- I always forget it. But anyway, it has to do something with hardware, not software.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I don't know if you mentioned what Dan does.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dan is a physician, basically. And he is a-- he's a professor of medicine at Vanderbilt. And he is a-- he's director of the clinical pharmacology division-- division of clinical pharmacology and cardiology.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do the-- are the children all aware of the fact that you volunteered for this interview today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They are all aware. And they were all for it. And they prodded me to do it. I must say that, as I told you, Paul was very unhappy that he couldn't be present at the interview. And I think they all would have liked to be here. But they are OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, on reflecting on everything that happened to you, what message would you want to give to your children, and grandchildren, and the other generations to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What message? I don't know. Stay strong and true to yourself. And try to muddle through. Do your best. That's about it. Because I feel it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't survive because of something special. We survived because we were lucky, you know, that we-- I told you, with the transport. We could have gone in September. And there was no-- no way to get out of it. We could have decided that we'll stay longer in Terezin, which we could have at that point, as many of our friends did. And maybe we wouldn't have survived, you know. Maybe we would have not gone straight to the gas. And maybe we would have gone to a selection. That I don't know. But with the September transport, I know. So it's definitely Lady Luck a little bit. And then, maybe it's-- maybe that you learn how to look a little bit over your shoulder, that you see the danger before-- before the next person, you know, that you sort of develop a seventh sense there-- this might not be right to you But without-- without the lucky breaks that we had, we couldn't have survived. And once you had the lucky breaks, you had to do something for it. You had to-- if you didn't have enough to eat, you have to learn how to ration your food-- a little-- always a little bit of something-- some sort of an input, I think. And-- like if you-- that's-- that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you for honoring us with your memories today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you are very welcome. I'm glad I could do it. I really am glad-- not for myself, but mainly for the kids. I-- I had-- I mean, the big children, like Dan's children, they-- mostly the boys. I don't think Claire is aware of it yet. They know about it. And Claire will be a little bit aware of it, because we are taking her to Prague in June, because we promised. We took her brothers two or three years ago. And so now, it's her turn. And the little ones and the-- and then their children, for them, it's-- it's important maybe to learn what-- what man's inhumanity to man can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eva, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are welcome. I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, can you tell me what we're looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you are looking at my diary, which I started to write on-- in November of 1939, and wrote practically until 1940, 1941. I don't remember the date there. And we-- and then I-- it was hidden with some books, with some books of our family. And I got it back after the war. But I never wrote into it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house where I lived in Terezin. The arrow is pointing to the window. And basically, it hasn't changed in 50 years except for the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind this window is the small office where we got married on November 22, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Terezin. I said it, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken from the inscription of the memorial wall in Prague after old Pinkas Synagogue, where they have all the names of all the citizens of Czech Republic who perished in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any particular name that we see?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's written-- Kamila?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, here-- Vonchovska, Kamila.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date beside her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's her birth date. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Ruda's grandfather's house in [NON-ENGLISH]. OK. When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three years ago. They are Ruda's camp buddies who are our friends now in Canada. That's enough now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my registration after we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that say, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says Central Registry Transport, my name, and Returned. You know, Ruda translated it already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who issued this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish-- I think the Central Jewish Registry in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, what are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are looking at the-- the shoes and part of the dress that I got as my first civilian clothes after the liberation in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest son, Dan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our son, Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Mischa with our granddaughter, Sasha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our 50th wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruda and Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Noah and Sasha in Boston, in May, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Scott, Mischa, and Noah, on November 22, 199-- what was it when we-- when-- what-- the-- the anniversary? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our family free picture taken in Nashville, Tennessee, on November 22, 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who's in the picture going from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's Claire and Maisie, Mischa and Noah, Ruda and Eva, Peter, Patty, Daniella, Scott, Dan, Mark, and Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, again, November 22, 1992 Maisie and Mischa.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Noah and Daniella, November 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: June in 1993, Prague, Eva, Paul, Mark, and Ruda.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech mountains, in June 1993 with Zdena, and Adia, Ruda, Paul, Eva, and Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bohemia, in the country with Mark, Ruda, Paul, and Eva, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a book that we wrote in Galveston, in 1984, about our adventures in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank--
